critique.summary: The article claims that the open problem related to improving lower bounds on a sequence H(n) in Ramsey theory has been solved using GPT-5.4 Pro, with confirmation from a contributing mathematician, Will Brian. The solution is planned to be written up for publication, and other AI models have also solved the problem using a developed testing scaffold.
mainPoints: Kevin Barreto and Liam Price used GPT-5.4 Pro to find a solution to a frontier math open problem involving hypergraphs and Ramsey theory.
Will Brian, a contributor to the problem, confirmed the solution and expressed interest in publishing it and exploring follow-on work.
Other AI models such as Opus 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and GPT-5.4 (xhigh) also solved the problem using a general testing scaffold.
The problem involves improving lower bounds on H(n), related to partitions in hypergraphs, which has been a challenging open problem in mathematics.
The solution and related AI interactions are documented and will be updated with future publications.
critique.truthfulness: The claim that GPT-5.4 Pro solved a known open problem in Ramsey theory is extraordinary and currently lacks independent verification from established mathematical journals or multiple independent expert sources. While the article states that a contributing mathematician confirmed the solution and plans to publish it, there is no evidence yet of peer-reviewed publication or broader community acceptance. The involvement of multiple AI models in solving the problem is plausible given recent advances in AI-assisted mathematics, but the claim should be treated cautiously until formal publication and peer review occur.
considerations: The article does not provide direct links to the full solution or peer-reviewed publications verifying the claim.
No independent expert opinions or external validations beyond the contributor Will Brian are presented.
The complexity and significance of the problem suggest that independent verification by the mathematical community is necessary before accepting the claim.
The article does not discuss potential limitations or errors in the AI-generated solution.
The broader implications for AI in mathematical research and the reproducibility of the solution by other researchers are not addressed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the significance of GPT-5.4 Pro solving a frontier math problem, with commenters debating the implications for AI capabilities in mathematics, the reliability of problem difficulty assessments, and the potential for AI to contribute to novel research. Some express cautious optimism about AI's role in advancing mathematical proofs, while others question the current utility and future impact.

comments.author: vlinx
comments.text: This is a remarkable result if confirmed independently. The gap between solving competition problems and open research problems has always been significant - bridging that gap suggests something qualitatively different in the model capabilities.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately highlights the significance of the achievement and the need for independent verification, reflecting a realistic and informed perspective on AI progress in research.

comments.author: renewiltord
comments.text: Fantastic news! That means with the right support tooling existing models are already capable of solving novel mathematics. There’s probably a lot of good mathematics out there we are going to make progress on.
comments.analysis: The comment is optimistic but grounded, recognizing the potential of AI models combined with tooling to advance mathematical research, which aligns with current trends in AI-assisted proof development.

comments.author: pinkmuffinere
comments.text:
As someone with only passing exposure to serious math, this section was by far the most interesting to me:

> The author assessed the problem as follows.

> [number of mathematicians familiar, number trying, how long an expert would take, how notable, etc]

How reliably can we know these things a-priori? Are these mostly guesses? I don't mean to diminish the value of guesses; I'm curious how reliable these kinds of guesses are.
comments.analysis: Raises a valid and intelligent question about the reliability of difficulty assessments in frontier math problems, reflecting a critical approach to evaluating the claims made by the original article.

comments.author: 6thbit
comments.text:
> Subsequent to this solve, we finished developing our general scaffold for testing models on FrontierMath: Open Problems. In this scaffold, several other models were able to solve the problem as well: Opus 4.6 (max), Gemini 3.1 Pro, and GPT-5.4 (xhigh).

Interesting. Whats that “scaffold”? A sort of unit test framework for proofs?
comments.analysis: This comment seeks clarification on the methodology used, indicating engagement and a desire to understand the technical framework behind the results.

comments.author: johnfn
comments.text: I like to imagine that the number of consumed tokens before a solution is found is a proxy for how difficult a problem is, and it looks like Opus 4.6 consumed around 250k tokens. That means that a tricky React refactor I did earlier today at work was about half as hard as an open problem in mathematics! :)
comments.analysis: Offers an interesting heuristic relating token consumption to problem difficulty, which is plausible but not rigorously validated, making it a reasonable but speculative insight.

comments.author: osti
comments.text: Seems like the high compute parallel thinking models weren't even needed, both the  normal 5.4 and gemini 3.1 pro solved it. Somehow Gemini 3 deepthink couldn't solve it.
comments.analysis: Provides a nuanced observation about model performance differences, which could be accurate but lacks detailed evidence or explanation.

comments.author: tombert
comments.text:
I was trying to get Claude and Codex to try and write a proof in Isabelle for the Collatz conjecture, but annoyingly it didn't solve it, and I don't feel like I'm any closer than I was when I started. AI is useless!

In all seriousness, this is pretty cool. I suspect that there's a lot of theoretical math that haven't been solved simply because of the "size" of the proof. An AI feedback loop into something like Isabelle or Lean does seem like it could end up opening up a lot of proofs.
comments.analysis: Expresses mixed feelings, initially dismissive but then optimistic about AI's potential in handling large proofs, reflecting common ambivalence in the community.

comments.author: daveguy
comments.text:
New goalpost, and I promise I'm not being facetious at all, genuinely curious:

Can an AI pose an frontier math problem that is of any interest to mathematicians?

I would guess 1) AI can solve frontier math problems and 2) can pose interesting/relevant math problems together would be an "oh shit" moment. Because that would be true PhD level research.
comments.analysis: Raises a forward-looking and insightful question about AI's ability not just to solve but to generate meaningful research problems, highlighting a key frontier in AI research.

comments.author: karmasimida
comments.text: No denial at this point, AI could produce something novel, and they will be doing more of this moving forward.
comments.analysis: Makes a general but reasonable statement about AI's growing role in producing novel outputs, consistent with observed trends.

comments.author: an0malous
comments.text: I feel like there’s a fork in our future approaching where we’ll either blossom into a paradise for all or live under the thumb of like 5 immortal VCs
comments.analysis: Expresses a dramatic and speculative view on the societal impact of AI, more emotional and less grounded in the technical discussion.

comments.author: alberth
comments.text:
For those, like me, who find the prompt itself of interest …

> A full transcript of the original conversation with GPT-5.4 Pro can be found here [0] and GPT-5.4 Pro’s write-up from the end of that transcript can be found here [1].

[0] https://epoch.ai/files/open-problems/gpt-5-4-pro-hypergraph-...

[1] https://epoch.ai/files/open-problems/hypergraph-ramsey-gpt-5...
comments.analysis: Provides useful links but does not contribute substantive analysis, making it informative but not analytically valuable.
